metrics.narrative: RTX's business problem is indirect procurement fragmentation across Enterprise Services (50% of indirect spend) and Operational Procurement (remaining 50%), with direct parts spend siloed in three major business units (Raytheon, Pratt & Whitney, Collins Aerospace). The Next Step log from 12 Jan 2026 documents this structure and notes a multiyear project to consolidate procurement/sourcing for direct, though SAP S4 rollout will compete for resources. The compelling event is the EY-led 8-week transformation assessment for non-product (indirect) procurement, targeting the Enterprise Services team, which creates a window for Zycus to embed as the AI layer. However, no quantified savings target, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement, or ROI has been articulated by the buyer across available evidence. The Next Step references autonomous sourcing presented to the relevant stakeholder (EY) on 12 Jan 2026 as 'well received,' suggesting AI-driven efficiency is valued, but the buyer has not stated a specific financial or operational metric (e.g., '20% spend under management improvement' or '$5M annual savings'). Cecil's scope is EMEA/APAC indirect, not global, limiting the immediate addressable pain. The gap: quantifying the value case is the single highest-leverage move to convert POC interest into EB sponsorship.

champion.narrative: Cecil Robichon (Non-Product Procurement Director International, EMEA & APAC Regions) is the identified champion with moderate strength and recent engagement but limited authority. Cecil holds the Champion contact role and has driven all buyer-side activity: organized the 04 Feb 2026 demo, requested specific use-case data (PPG, Grainger, GMRE scenarios on 29 Jan 2026), proposed POC framing on 13 Mar 2026, and maintained regular communication (Next Step log shows Cecil engagement on 23 Sep 2025, 29 Sep 2025, 14 Oct 2025, 07 Nov 2025, 12 Jan 2026, 29 Jan 2026, 04 Feb 2026, 13 Mar 2026). However, Cecil explicitly stated he is 'NOT the global decision maker' (29 Jan 2026 Next Step), limiting his ability to commit budget or advance the deal past POC without VP and CFO sponsorship. His access to the Economic Buyer (CFO Neil Mitchill) and Decision Maker (VP Darrell Wilson) is unconfirmed; no evidence of Cecil introducing Zycus to either executive. His advocacy is demonstrated by the POC push ('possible POC with limited data to compare with Accenture's result,' 13 Mar 2026) and by navigating the VP hold-release cycle (07 Nov 2025: VP instructed hold, Cecil scheduled February reconnect). Current engagement status: active as of last buyer touch 22 Jun 2026 (14 days ago per engagement pulse), but the absence of EB/VP meetings post-Q1 2026 suggests Cecil has not escalated internally. Risk: champion is strong within his EMEA/APAC scope but may be insufficient to drive global vendor selection without executive co-sponsorship. The move: empower Cecil to co-sponsor a VP meeting post-POC, positioning him as the operational owner who validates the business case while the VP commits budget.

competition.narrative: RTX is benchmarking Zycus against three competitors: Accenture (incumbent custom spend analytics tool, primary threat), SAP Ariba, and Coupa. Accenture is the most immediate competitive risk. The 13 Mar 2026 Next Step references 'possible POC with limited data to compare with Accenture's result,' confirming Accenture is the baseline RTX will use to evaluate Zycus. Accenture's strength is incumbency and custom fit, but the POC framing implies dissatisfaction with accuracy, automation, or AI capability (otherwise no POC would be needed). The wedge: Zycus Merlin (agentic AI) for autonomous sourcing and ML iAnalyze for 97-100% spend classification accuracy, demonstrated live against Accenture's historical output on RTX's own data (PPG, Grainger, GMRE categories). SAP Ariba and Coupa are listed in the Competitors Salesforce field but have no supporting evidence in Next Step log or known calls, suggesting they are tracked competitors (possibly in scope for direct parts or enterprise-wide intake per 12 Jan 2026 discussion of 'Intake (enterprise wide) while interesting, would likely be taken up entirely by IT and be super scrutinized') but not active in the current indirect/EMEA evaluation. The 12 Jan 2026 Next Step confirms direct parts sourcing would 'sideline Zycus (FedRamp),' removing SAP Ariba and Coupa as threats in that scope. The competitive position: Zycus is well-positioned in the AI/Merlin lane (autonomous sourcing pitch to the relevant stakeholder was 'well received' on 12 Jan 2026) but must prove superiority over Accenture in the POC to win. If the POC does not launch or delivers marginal improvement over Accenture, RTX defaults to the incumbent.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is indirect procurement fragmentation, manual spend classification, and lack of AI-driven sourcing automation across a decentralized organization. The 12 Jan 2026 Next Step documents the structural pain: Enterprise Services team holds ~50% of indirect spend, Operational Procurement holds the other ~50%, and direct parts spend is siloed in three major business units (Raytheon, Pratt & Whitney, Collins Aerospace), creating visibility and control gaps. The EY 8-week assessment for non-product (indirect) procurement signals executive-level acknowledgment of the problem. The pain owner is the VP for Non-Product Procurement & Logistics, who authorized the EY engagement and instructed the vendor evaluation hold/release cycle (07 Nov 2025 Next Step). Cecil Robichon (EMEA/APAC director) owns the pain regionally but lacks global authority (29 Jan 2026 Next Step). The 29 Jan 2026 request for PPG, Grainger, and GMRE scenarios indicates operational pain around supplier visibility and category-specific analytics (GMRE is high-volume, low-value tail spend where manual classification creates procurement inefficiency). The 13 Mar 2026 POC framing ('compare with Accenture's result') confirms dissatisfaction with the incumbent tool's accuracy or automation. The gap: no quantified impact (cost of fragmentation, hours lost to manual classification, error rate, savings leakage) has been stated by the buyer. The move: use the POC to surface quantified pain (e.g., 'Accenture achieves 85% classification accuracy; Zycus delivers 97-100%, saving 200 hours/month and $2M annually in on-contract compliance').

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics (MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement channel) are undocumented. No evidence of InfoSec kickoff, redlining discussions, or ERP/systems integration scoping in Next Step log, contact roles, or available evidence. The FedRAMP concern (12 Jan 2026 Next Step: 'FedRamp may come up and we will walk quickly away if firm requirement...agreed direct parts sourcing would likely sideline Zycus (FedRamp)') suggests a cyber/compliance gate exists, but the 04 Feb 2026 action item to 'confirm security compliance requirements with the RTX cyber team regarding Zycus's FedRAMP and NIST controls' has no follow-up evidence of resolution. If RTX cyber requires FedRAMP for indirect scope (not only defense/direct), the deal is blocked; if ISO 27001 + SOC 1/2 + NIST is acceptable, the path is clear, but this has not been confirmed. No SI/partner implementation role is defined beyond EY's advisory function. The SAP S4 rollout referenced in the 12 Jan 2026 Next Step may create integration touchpoints (Zycus ingesting SAP spend data), but scoping is absent. Current Contract Expiration (if Accenture has a term) is unknown. The gap: paper-process visibility requires a contracting and InfoSec deep-dive call post-POC to surface MSA requirements, data residency constraints, and cyber approval timeline. Without this, the close-date math is unreliable.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a gated enterprise motion influenced by EY advisors and the VP hold-release cycle. The VP for Non-Product Procurement & Logistics imposed a hold until mid-Q1 2026 (07 Nov 2025 Next Step), which was lifted with the 04 Feb 2026 demo. The Process & Tools team (Emily Morvan, Eliot Vilain) reviews vendor fit, per Next Step references. EY partners Brad Durst (indirect transformation) and Sam Murphy (direct COTS components) are external advisors running an 8-week assessment (12 Jan 2026 Next Step), and their recommendation carries weight ('well received' autonomous sourcing pitch suggests EY endorsement is achievable). The approval chain is multi-tiered: Cecil (champion, EMEA/APAC director) cannot commit budget (29 Jan Next Step), the VP for Non-Product Procurement & Logistics controls operational approval (07 Nov Next Step), and Neil Mitchill (CFO, Executive Sponsor contact role) holds final financial authority. However, the actual sequence (POC → VP validation → CFO sign-off → contracting) is inferred, not documented. The Next Step log shows no formal milestone gate (e.g., 'Shortlist decision by [date]' or 'CFO review scheduled'). The 13 Mar 2026 Next Step proposes two parallel tracks: POC and 'discuss beyond spend / transformation involvement,' suggesting the process may expand scope mid-evaluation. The risk: without a published timeline and approval gates, the deal drifts. The move: secure a three-way call with Cecil, EY advisors, and Zycus to align on transformation milestones and lock the process map (POC → EY endorsement → VP approval → CFO validation → contract).

flags: POC design is the single highest-leverage near-term action. Accenture is the POC benchmark (Next Step 13 Mar 2026: 'possible POC with limited data to compare with Accenture's result'). The POC must be surgical: use RTX's live spend data (PPG, Grainger, GMRE categories per their 29 Jan request), demonstrate 97-100% classification accuracy vs. Accenture's manual baseline, automate on-contract visibility and GMRE tagging, and deliver clean results in days (not the April/May timeline Cecil cited as 'outrageous'). Co-author the POC scope with Cecil and the Process & Tools team (Emily Morvan, Eliot Vilain) to ensure it tests their exact pain points. A winning POC is the wedge that makes Accenture indefensible and unlocks VP approval.

EY transformation alignment is an under-leveraged forcing function. The Next Step log (12 Jan 2026) shows EY (Brad Durst for indirect, Sam Murphy for direct) is running an 8-week assessment of RTX's indirect procurement and advising on the 'One-RTX' supply chain transformation. Cecil has been trying to coordinate with EY ('continue to chase the relevant stakeholder team leads for debrief - and Brad has been quiet' per Next Step 13 Mar 2026). EY's endorsement or integration of Zycus into their transformation roadmap would de-risk the vendor selection and create urgency (transformation timeline as forcing function). The move: ask Cecil to set a three-way call (Zycus, Cecil, EY) to position Zycus as the AI-driven spend analytics layer that accelerates EY's transformation milestones (faster clean data → faster savings identification → faster One-RTX procurement harmonization). If EY recommends Zycus, the VP and CFO follow.

FedRAMP certification flagged as 'could be a showstopper' by Cecil on 8 Sep 2025. Zycus is ISO 27001 + SOC 1/2 certified with US/EU data centers and NIST controls but not FedRAMP certified. 4 Feb 2026 team demo action item: 'Confirm security compliance requirements with the RTX cyber team regarding Zycus's FedRAMP and NIST controls.' Status: unresolved. Risk: if RTX cyber requires FedRAMP for indirect/non-product scope (not only defense/direct), the deal is blocked. Mitigation: clarify with Cecil and the cyber team whether ISO + NIST is acceptable for this scope, or whether FedRAMP is a hard gate. Next Step log (12 Jan 2026) notes FedRAMP concern may 'sideline Zycus' for direct parts sourcing, suggesting it may not be a blocker for indirect.

EY (Brad Durst, Sam Murphy) is advising on RTX's indirect procurement transformation and running an 8-week assessment. EY's endorsement could accelerate the deal; lack of EY engagement is a risk. Next Step 13 Mar 2026: 'continue to chase the relevant stakeholder team leads (Brad for indirect and Sam Murphy for direct) for debrief - and Brad has been quiet.' Risk: if EY does not recommend Zycus or delays their assessment, the vendor selection stalls. Mitigation: secure a three-way call (Zycus, Cecil, EY) to position Zycus as the AI layer that enables EY's transformation milestones.

Close date 15 Feb 2027 is optimistic given 234 days remaining and enterprise motion requirements (POC, EB sign-off, contract negotiation, InfoSec review). Deal has been in Qualified stage for 292 days with no progression to Shortlisted or Vendor Selected. Risk: timeline slips to Q3 or Q4 2027. Mitigation: compress POC timeline (target 4-6 weeks), secure VP approval immediately post-POC, and run contracting in parallel with final EB validation.

POC scope and execution not yet launched. Next Step 13 Mar 2026 references 'possible POC with limited data to compare with Accenture's result,' but no evidence of POC kickoff, data shared, or timeline agreed. Risk: POC delays or scope drift. Mitigation: co-author POC scope with Cecil and Process & Tools team (Emily, Eliot) in next call, agree on data (PPG, Grainger, GMRE scenarios per 29 Jan request), timeline (4-6 weeks), and success criteria (97-100% classification accuracy, on-contract visibility, GMRE automation).

POC design is the single highest-leverage near-term action. Accenture is the POC benchmark (Next Step 13 Mar 2026: 'possible POC with limited data to compare with Accenture's result'). The POC must be surgical: use RTX's live spend data (PPG, Grainger, GMRE categories per their 29 Jan request), demonstrate 97-100% classification accuracy vs. Accenture's manual baseline, automate on-contract visibility and GMRE tagging, and deliver clean results in days (not the April/May timeline Cecil cited as 'outrageous'). Co-author the POC scope with Cecil and the Process & Tools team (Emily Morvan, Eliot Vilain) to ensure it tests their exact pain points. A winning POC is the wedge that makes Accenture indefensible and unlocks VP approval.

Executive air cover is missing and must be built now. The VP for Non-Product Procurement & Logistics (the approval authority per Next Step 7 Nov 2025) and Neil Mitchill (CFO, Executive Sponsor per Salesforce Contact Role) have not appeared on any call or been directly engaged in 9 months. Cecil is a strong champion but explicitly stated he is 'NOT the global decision maker' and 'I'm not going to be the one to convince' (8 Sep 2025). The next gate (POC → contract) requires VP and CFO buy-in. The move: ask Cecil to co-sponsor a 30-minute meeting with the VP to align on POC scope, business case, and timeline, positioning it as a transformation enabler (the 'One-RTX' supply chain initiative referenced in Next Step). Then introduce the CFO (Neil Mitchill) for strategic and financial validation, leveraging Cecil's GE reference and the POC results as proof points. Without exec engagement, the deal stays in Qualified indefinitely.

EY transformation alignment is an under-leveraged forcing function. The Next Step log (12 Jan 2026) shows EY (Brad Durst for indirect, Sam Murphy for direct) is running an 8-week assessment of RTX's indirect procurement and advising on the 'One-RTX' supply chain transformation. Cecil has been trying to coordinate with EY ('continue to chase the relevant stakeholder team leads for debrief - and Brad has been quiet' per Next Step 13 Mar 2026). EY's endorsement or integration of Zycus into their transformation roadmap would de-risk the vendor selection and create urgency (transformation timeline as forcing function). The move: ask Cecil to set a three-way call (Zycus, Cecil, EY) to position Zycus as the AI-driven spend analytics layer that accelerates EY's transformation milestones (faster clean data → faster savings identification → faster One-RTX procurement harmonization). If EY recommends Zycus, the VP and CFO follow.
